keyIdeas: Oxidation number (oxidation state) is a formal charge assigned to an atom in a compound using a set of rules; it tracks electron transfer in redox reactions.

Rules (in priority order): free elements = 0; monatomic ions = charge; O is −2 (except in peroxides −1 and OF₂ +2); H is +1 with non-metals (−1 in metal hydrides); F is always −1; sum of oxidation numbers = overall charge of species.
Oxidation is an increase in oxidation number (loss of electrons — OIL); reduction is a decrease in oxidation number (gain of electrons — RIG).
An oxidising agent gains electrons and is itself reduced; a reducing agent loses electrons and is itself oxidised.
Oxidation numbers are used to name compounds containing transition metals: iron(III) chloride has Fe in +3; copper(II) sulfate has Cu in +2.
Disproportionation is a reaction in which the same element is simultaneously oxidised and reduced.

mustKnow.definition: A formal integer (or fraction) representing the hypothetical charge an atom would have if all bonds were ionic; determined by the priority rules.
An increase in oxidation number; loss of electrons. Remembered as OIL (Oxidation Is Loss).
A decrease in oxidation number; gain of electrons. Remembered as RIG (Reduction Is Gain).
A species that oxidises another substance by accepting electrons; the oxidising agent itself is reduced (its oxidation number decreases).
A species that reduces another substance by donating electrons; the reducing agent itself is oxidised (its oxidation number increases).
A redox reaction in which the same element in the same species is simultaneously oxidised and reduced.

markSchemePhrases: OIL RIG (oxidation is loss, reduction is gain)
oxidising agent is reduced / gains electrons
reducing agent is oxidised / loses electrons
sum of oxidation numbers equals overall charge
disproportionation — same element oxidised AND reduced
commandWords: 'Identify' the oxidising/reducing agent — name the substance and state its change in oxidation number. 'State' whether a reaction is redox — look for a change in oxidation number. 'Explain' why a reaction is disproportionation — show the same element increasing and decreasing in oxidation number. 'Deduce' oxidation numbers — show your working using the priority rules.
examinerTips: Always show the working when deducing an oxidation number: state the rule applied and the arithmetic (e.g. 'sum = 0, O = −2 × 4, so Mn = +7 in MnO₄⁻ after accounting for the −1 ion charge').
Watch the overall charge of the species: for MnO₄⁻ the sum of oxidation numbers = −1, not 0.
For disproportionation questions, explicitly show two different oxidation number changes for the same element.
Naming: 'iron(III) oxide' means Fe is +3; the Roman numeral IS the oxidation number — never write 'iron(3+)'.
The exception list is short: O is −2 except in H₂O₂ (peroxides, −1) and OF₂ (+2); H is +1 except in NaH (metal hydrides, −1).
workedExample:
Assign oxidation numbers to all atoms in K₂Cr₂O₇ and state the oxidation state of chromium.

Rules applied:
K: always +1 (Group 1 metal)
O: always −2 (no peroxide or fluoride context)

Let Cr = x.
Sum of oxidation numbers = 0 (neutral compound):
2(+1) + 2(x) + 7(−2) = 0
2 + 2x − 14 = 0
2x = 12
x = +6

Cr is in the +6 oxidation state in K₂Cr₂O₇.
commonTraps: Forgetting the overall charge of ions: treating MnO₄⁻ as if the sum is 0 instead of −1.
Saying the reducing agent 'gains electrons' — it loses electrons (it is oxidised).
Confusing 'oxidising agent' with 'the substance being oxidised' — the oxidising agent is itself reduced.
Assigning H as −1 in water (it is +1; −1 applies only in metal hydrides such as NaH).
Writing 'oxygen always has −2' without noting the peroxide and OF₂ exceptions — OCR mark schemes penalise unqualified absolute statements.
